title: Petrarch
summary: The Italian poet and scholar who invented the concept of historical distance — the idea that ancient Rome was a distinct civilization that had ended, not simply a continuous past — and whose recovery of classical texts and sense of historical belatedness made the Renaissance possible as an intellectual project.
hook: Before Petrarch, medieval Europeans thought they were Romans — or at least the heirs of an unbroken tradition. After Petrarch, they understood they were not. He invented the idea that antiquity was over: that it had been a distinct civilization, different from his own, whose loss could be mourned and whose recovery could be attempted. That invention is the Renaissance.

beginner.theFigure:
In 1345, a scholar rifling through a cathedral library in Verona found a bundle of old letters. They had been written by Cicero, one of ancient Rome's greatest speakers, and they had been lost for nearly a thousand years. The man who found them wept. His name was Francesco Petrarca, known to us as Petrarch, and his grief at finding those letters tells you almost everything about him.

Petrarch lived in Italy in the 1300s. He was a poet and a scholar. He wrote beautiful love poems in Italian about a woman named Laura. But the thing that made him matter most was not his poetry. It was the way he looked at the past.

Petrarch looked at ancient Rome and felt something that very few people before him had felt. He felt that it was gone. He felt that the world of Cicero and Virgil was a different world, a lost world. That feeling sounds simple. But it was not simple at all. It changed how people thought about history and the past for hundreds of years to come.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Petrarch grew up inside two very different worlds at once. One world was medieval Christianity. He was a sincere Christian. He read the Church Fathers, he thought about salvation, and he believed that earthly life was preparation for eternity.

The other world was ancient Rome. Petrarch read the Roman writers with a kind of hunger. He loved Cicero, Virgil, and Livy. He admired their writing. He admired their values. He thought they had something his own era had lost.

These two worlds pulled against each other. Christianity taught that earthly achievements mattered less than spiritual ones. But Petrarch could not stop admiring the pagan Romans. He wrote a long imaginary conversation between himself and the Christian writer Augustine, arguing both sides. He could not resolve it. That lasting tension is what makes him so interesting.
beginner.centralIdea:
Petrarch's biggest idea was that different times in history are genuinely different from each other. The past is not just a longer version of the present. It is a separate world that must be understood on its own terms.

Before Petrarch, most educated people in Europe did not think this way. They read ancient Roman texts as if they still applied directly to their own time. The Roman Church still used Latin. The Holy Roman Empire still claimed to be Roman. The past felt continuous and close.

Petrarch was among the earliest people to call the centuries after Rome's fall a dark age. He meant that something valuable had been lost and had not yet been recovered. That idea created something new: the concept that history has distinct periods. When people talk about ancient history, the Middle Ages, and the modern world, they are using a way of dividing up time that Petrarch helped create.
beginner.howSpread:
Petrarch did not start a school or give lectures. He spread his ideas through letters. He wrote to rulers, scholars, church officials, and friends. He also wrote letters to dead Roman authors, as if they could read them. He wrote to Cicero. He wrote to Livy. He told them what he thought of their work and shared his admiration.

This practice modeled something genuinely new: treating the ancient writers as real intellectual companions rather than just as distant authorities to quote from.

The scholars who came after Petrarch built on his work. Boccaccio, his friend and fellow writer, helped preserve and spread his ideas. A generation of scholars went on to find more lost ancient manuscripts, translate them, and study them carefully. This movement is called humanism. It grew into the Renaissance, the great flowering of art, literature, and thought in Europe from roughly the 1400s onward.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Petrarch's dark age idea was not a neutral historical description. It was a way of criticizing his own time by praising ancient Rome. Modern historians have pointed out that the medieval centuries were not actually dark. Gothic cathedrals, universities, and works like Dante's poetry all came from those very centuries. Petrarch's harsh label stuck in popular speech long after historians stopped using it.

His love poetry has also drawn criticism. Laura, the woman in his sonnets, is described in beautiful detail. But she does not speak or act as a full person. She works more like a mirror for Petrarch's own feelings. Critics have noted that the tradition of love poetry he inspired often treated women as objects to be admired rather than as full human beings with their own voices and lives.

general.theFigure:
When Petrarch found Cicero's letters to Atticus in a Verona cathedral library in 1345, he was not just pleased by a literary discovery. He was shaken. The letters showed a Cicero who was anxious, politically compromised, and deeply human. This was very different from the idealized and venerated moral sage that medieval readers had come to imagine. The gap between the Cicero Petrarch had expected and the one the letters revealed was the gap between the past as a comforting story and the past as a real, irrecoverable world. That gap became his central subject.

Petrarch was a fourteenth-century Italian poet and scholar. He is remembered for his Italian love sonnets and his Latin writings on classical culture. His lasting contribution, though, was the idea of historical distance: the sense that antiquity was a distinct civilization that had ended, that grieving its loss was an appropriate response, and that patient scholarship might recover at least part of what had been lost.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Medieval readers mostly used classical texts as quarries. They dug out passages that could confirm Christian teaching, gathered them into anthologies, and read them filtered through commentaries. Few scholars read classical authors whole, as full human beings writing for a specific world.

Petrarch changed this approach. He insisted on reading the ancient authors in their full context, attending to what they actually said rather than what they could be made to mean. This shift, from mining texts for useful quotations to reading them carefully and completely, became one foundation of Renaissance humanism as a scholarly practice.

Yet Petrarch was also a sincere Christian who read Augustine with the same intensity he brought to Cicero. His Secretum is an imaginary dialogue between himself and Augustine. It does not resolve the tension between classical learning and Christian piety. Instead it dramatizes the conflict and leaves it unresolved. Petrarch saw that the two worlds he loved could not easily be squared with each other. He chose to stay inside the tension rather than explain it away. That unusual honesty gives his work a quality of self-examination that readers have found compelling across many centuries.
general.centralIdea:
Petrarch's central contribution was the idea of historical periods: the claim that history divides into distinct eras, each with its own character. Before this idea took hold, educated Europeans experienced antiquity largely as continuous background rather than as a lost world. The Roman Church, the Holy Roman Empire, and the Latin language all suggested an unbroken line back to Rome. Petrarch was among the earliest to feel that apparent continuity as a fiction. He experienced the distance between his own era and antiquity as a real, grievable loss.

In giving the post-Roman centuries the label of a dark age, he also gave birth to the concept of the Middle Ages as a distinct historical period. The people living through those centuries did not think of themselves as medieval. That category required someone to define the ancient and the modern first. Once those two poles were set, the medieval was what fell in between. The three-part division of history that now seems obvious was not a natural discovery. It was Petrarch's construction, shaped by his sense of what had been lost.
general.howSpread:
Petrarch's influence spread mainly through letters. He cultivated relationships with rulers, scholars, and church officials across Italy and beyond, and his letters circulated widely among educated readers of his time.

Among his most striking letters were ones addressed directly to Cicero, Livy, and other ancient authors, treating them as absent friends rather than remote authorities. This was an unusual way to engage with the dead. It modeled a new relationship with the classical past, one that was personal and imaginative rather than formal and deferential.

After his death, Boccaccio helped secure Petrarch's standing by preserving and promoting his work. The Petrarchan sonnet form spread through manuscript copies and then through print, and it became a widely used verse form in European poetry over the following centuries. Later poets including Wyatt, Sidney, and Shakespeare all worked within the conventions Petrarch had established. Shakespeare's Sonnet 130, which gently mocks the habits of idealized description, gets its point across only because those habits were already so familiar to readers. You cannot subvert a convention that has not yet been established.
general.contestedInheritance:
One line of criticism argues that the Petrarchan love tradition does something more troubling than simple idealization. The convention tends to depersonalize women, using the beloved as a surface for the poet's own self-examination rather than portraying her as a person with her own inner life. Laura in the sonnets is rendered as a list of beautiful parts: eyes, hair, hands, each described carefully, none adding up to a speaking subject. The tradition Petrarch established carried those assumptions forward through centuries of European love poetry, shaping how writers imagined both love and women.

Historians of medieval culture have also questioned whether the dark ages image works as historical analysis. Petrarch coined it as a polemical contrast, not as careful description. Gothic architecture, scholastic philosophy, and Dante's Comedy all came from the very centuries he was dismissing. Later scholarship has shown that his dark age was a rhetorical construction, built to serve his own sense of cultural loss rather than to describe what the medieval world actually was. Whether a construction with such origins can still serve as a useful rough shorthand, and whether all historical periods are in some sense invented, is a question historians continue to debate.

advanced.theFigure: The most productive recent scholarship on Petrarch has focused on his specific intellectual practices rather than his general significance. Carol Quillen's Rereading the Renaissance examines how Petrarch read — the annotations he made in manuscripts, the quotations he collected in his commonplace books, the ways he absorbed and transformed his classical sources. This micro-level analysis reveals a thinker whose relationship to the classical past was more complex than the standard narrative of recovery allows: Petrarch did not simply find antiquity and transmit it; he constructed a version of antiquity that served his own purposes.
advanced.worldOfIdeas: The philological dimension of Petrarch's work — his attention to the material transmission of classical texts, his efforts to establish accurate texts by comparing manuscripts — gave Renaissance humanism its specific character as a discipline of reading rather than a set of received opinions. His discovery in 1345 of Cicero's letters to Atticus — previously unknown to the Middle Ages — was the most significant of many such finds. The letters revealed a Cicero who was politically engaged, anxious, and all too human: very different from the idealized figure of medieval tradition. This discovery shaped Petrarch's understanding of Cicero and, through him, the humanist ideal of the active civic life.
advanced.centralIdea:
Giuseppe Mazzotta's analysis of Petrarch's Secretum — his imaginary dialogue with Augustine — reveals the philosophical depth of Petrarch's engagement with the tension between classical and Christian culture. The dialogue is not resolved: Petrarch does not choose between Augustine (who urges him to renounce earthly attachments) and his own attachment to Laura and to fame. The irresolution is the point. Petrarch understood that the tension between classical and Christian values was real, not merely apparent, and that it could not be dissolved by philosophical argument.

This unresolved tension — the willingness to inhabit contradiction rather than resolving it through systematic theology — is what makes Petrarch a genuinely modern figure. His sense of internal division, of being divided between competing goods that cannot be reconciled, is a specifically modern experience that classical or high medieval thinkers did not characteristically express.
advanced.howSpread: The dissemination of Petrarch's influence through the humanist networks of the fifteenth century has been traced by Ronald Witt in In the Footsteps of the Ancients. Witt shows that the humanist movement was not simply Petrarch's personal influence extended outward but a broader cultural transformation in which Petrarch's work was one catalyst. The Florentine civic humanists — Salutati, Bruni, Bracciolini — extended his project in directions he had not anticipated, applying the recovery of classical rhetoric to political and historical writing in ways that transformed European political thought.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The question of Petrarch's modernity — whether he was the first modern person, as Burckhardt famously claimed, or a figure still embedded in medieval assumptions — has generated a substantial scholarly literature. Burckhardt's Renaissance individualism thesis, which made Petrarch its primary example, has been substantially qualified: Petrarch's individuality was expressed within, not against, the Christian framework, and the distinctively 'modern' aspects of his self-presentation were partly a literary construction rather than a transparent self-revelation.

What remains defensible is that Petrarch made a specific intellectual move — the invention of historical distance — that was genuinely consequential and genuinely new. Whether he was the first to make this move or the first to make it with sufficient clarity and force to be historically effective is a question about degree rather than kind.

foundation.theStory: Petrarch invented the concept of historical distance — the idea that antiquity was a different world that had ended, whose loss could be mourned and whose recovery could be attempted. This intellectual move created the Renaissance as a project: the systematic effort to recover classical learning and apply it to the present. Every time someone refers to the 'Middle Ages' they use a concept Petrarch created. Every time a historian tries to understand the past on its own terms rather than as a lesson for the present, they use a method he pioneered.
foundation.theDebate: Scholars debate whether Petrarch's historical consciousness was genuinely new or a more acute expression of tendencies already present in medieval culture; whether his periodization (ancient/medieval/modern) was a scholarly discovery or a rhetorical construction serving his own purposes; and whether the Renaissance he helped to initiate was a rupture with medieval culture or a continuation of it.
foundation.whyItMatters: Petrarch matters as the inventor of a way of thinking about the past that is now so deeply embedded in educated thinking that it seems natural: the idea that different historical periods have their own character, that the past must be understood on its own terms, and that understanding the past requires overcoming the distance that separates us from it. This is the foundation of modern historical scholarship, literary study, and the humanities broadly.
questions: Petrarch invented the 'dark ages' as a rhetorical concept — a way of expressing his admiration for antiquity and his dissatisfaction with his own time. How should historians evaluate historical periodizations that were invented for polemical purposes? Can a polemical construction also be a useful analytical tool?

Petrarch experienced the distance between himself and antiquity as loss — a grief for a world he could not fully recover. How does a sense of historical belatedness (the feeling that the best things happened before you) function as an intellectual motivation? What does it enable, and what does it distort?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The key scholarly works are Mommsen's essay on Petrarch and the dark ages, Baron's Crisis of the Early Italian Renaissance (on civic humanism), Witt's In the Footsteps of the Ancients (on the transmission of humanist influence), and Quillen's Rereading the Renaissance (on Petrarch's reading practices). Mazzotta's work on the Secretum addresses the philosophical dimensions. The feminist critique of Petrarchan love poetry (Jones, Vickers) has reshaped how the literary tradition is understood.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for Petrarch's intellectual work is exceptionally rich — his letters, his annotations in manuscripts, his poetry, his Latin prose works, and his Latin and vernacular correspondence survive in unusual completeness. The limits: the reconstruction of his influence requires tracing ideas through a network of texts and persons, which involves interpretive choices about what counts as Petrarchan influence versus independent development.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Petrarch must specify which of his contributions is being claimed as consequential. The invention of historical periodization, the development of philological method, the articulation of literary fame, and the Petrarchan love convention are distinct contributions with different genealogies and different significances. Arguments that treat 'Petrarch invented the Renaissance' as a simple claim miss the specific intellectual moves that made his work consequential.
questions: Petrarch's letters to Cicero, Livy, and other dead ancient authors treated them as contemporaries — absent friends whose works he had just read. What was the intellectual significance of this practice? What relationship to the past does it model, and how is it different from the medieval approach to authority?

The Renaissance humanists believed they were recovering antiquity. Modern historians believe they were constructing a version of antiquity that served their own purposes. Can both things be true? What is the relationship between the recovery and construction of the past?

research.topic: Petrarch's invention of the concept of the Dark Ages
research.content: Theodore Mommsen's 1942 essay 'Petrarch's Conception of the Dark Ages' established that Petrarch was the originator of the light/darkness metaphor for historical periodization — the idea that the post-classical centuries were a 'dark age' between the light of antiquity and the anticipated light of recovery. This essay is the foundational scholarly statement of Petrarch's historical significance and has not been superseded, though subsequent scholarship has complicated the claim by noting medieval precedents for some of Petrarch's historical thinking.

research.topic: The philological foundations of Renaissance humanism
research.content: Scholarly work on Petrarch's manuscript annotations, commonplace books, and editing practices has established that his approach to classical texts — reading them whole, attending to their context, comparing manuscripts to establish accurate texts — was foundational for the philological humanism of the Renaissance. Ronald Witt's work traces how this approach was transmitted to and transformed by Petrarch's successors. The philological tradition he helped establish is the ancestor of modern literary and historical scholarship.

Laura — the woman who inspired Petrarch's Italian sonnets — cannot be identified with certainty. The traditional identification with Laura de Noves, wife of Hugues de Sade, was proposed by later commentators and is not confirmed by any contemporary evidence. What evidence exists: Petrarch's notes in his copy of Virgil, which record meeting Laura on April 6, 1327, and her death on the same date in 1348; the poems themselves, which provide no identifying information. Whether Laura was a specific historical person, an idealized construction, or some combination cannot be established.

comparativeNarrative.name: Medieval Continuity Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: Historians of medieval culture have argued that Petrarch's dark ages label hid real achievements. Gothic cathedrals, university philosophy, and Dante's Comedy all came from those supposedly dark centuries. The medieval world preserved and reshaped classical culture in its own ways. Petrarch's rhetoric made that hard to see. When modern historians rehabilitated the Middle Ages, they were not proving Petrarch wrong so much as showing that his periodization was a polemical move. He was making a case for antiquity, not describing history as it was.

comparativeNarrative.name: Non-European Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: The way Petrarch thought about history, dividing time into periods, grieving a lost past, and trying to recover it through study, is not how everyone has approached the past. Islamic, Chinese, and Indian historical traditions each had their own ways of relating to earlier times. None of them were organized around the idea of a lost golden age to recover. The framework of ancient, medieval, and modern spread across the world not because it is the natural way to divide history. It spread because European political and cultural power carried it outward.

comparativeNarrative.name: Literary Tradition Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: The sonnet form Petrarch used in his Italian poems became the leading lyric form in European poetry for three centuries. Spanish, French, English, Portuguese, and other poets all adopted it. English poets from Wyatt and Surrey through Sidney and Shakespeare worked both within and against the convention Petrarch had set. Shakespeare's Sonnet 130 opens by saying his mistress's eyes are nothing like the sun. That joke only lands because readers knew exactly what idealized description looked like. You cannot argue against a tradition that does not yet exist.

rabbitHole.label: The Renaissance
rabbitHole.reason: The Renaissance was the flowering of the intellectual project Petrarch initiated — the recovery of classical learning and its application to art, literature, and thought.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: The Enlightenment's historical self-consciousness — its sense of itself as a distinct modern age in contrast to earlier superstition — was a development of the periodization Petrarch invented.

rabbitHole.label: Augustine of Hippo
rabbitHole.reason: Augustine is Petrarch's great interlocutor in the Secretum — the theological tradition that Petrarch loved and struggled with, embodied in the thinker he most admired among the Church Fathers.

rabbitHole.label: The Black Death
rabbitHole.reason: The Black Death killed Laura (possibly) and reshaped the world Petrarch lived in — the mortality and social disruption of the plague was the context within which his historical consciousness developed.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The historical consciousness Petrarch invented — the sense that a people has a past worth recovering and a future worth building — is one of the intellectual ancestors of modern nationalism.

reference.author: Mommsen, Theodore
reference.title: Petrarch's Conception of the 'Dark Ages'
reference.annotation: The foundational scholarly statement of what Petrarch actually invented — Mommsen's essay on the light/darkness metaphor for historical periodization is essential reading for understanding Petrarch's historical significance.

reference.author: Witt, Ronald G.
reference.title: In the Footsteps of the Ancients: The Origins of Humanism from Lovato to Bruni
reference.annotation: The essential account of how Petrarch's humanism was transmitted and transformed by his successors — Witt traces the development of humanist literary practice from Petrarch through the Florentine civic humanists.

reference.author: Baron, Hans
reference.title: The Crisis of the Early Italian Renaissance
reference.annotation: The essential account of civic humanism — Baron's argument that the Florentine humanists developed Petrarch's literary recovery into a civic and political program remains influential despite subsequent qualification.

reference.author: Quillen, Carol
reference.title: Rereading the Renaissance: Petrarch, Augustine, and the Language of Humanism
reference.annotation: The essential account of Petrarch's reading practices — Quillen's micro-level analysis of how Petrarch read and annotated texts reveals the complexity of his relationship to classical antiquity.

reference.author: Mann, Nicholas
reference.title: Petrarch
reference.annotation: The best concise scholarly introduction — Mann's account covers Petrarch's life, works, and significance with clarity and authority. The essential starting point for scholarly engagement.

reference.author: Burckhardt, Jacob
reference.title: The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy
reference.annotation: The foundational account of the Renaissance that made Petrarch central — Burckhardt's thesis of Renaissance individualism has been substantially qualified but remains the essential starting point for understanding the historical debate about Petrarch's significance.

reference.author: Mazzotta, Giuseppe
reference.title: The Worlds of Petrarch
reference.annotation: The source of the entry's reading of the Secretum as a deliberately unresolved dialogue with Augustine — Mazzotta's analysis grounds the claim that Petrarch's willingness to inhabit contradiction is what makes him a modern figure.

reference.author: Petrarch, Francesco
reference.title: Secretum (Secretum meum)
reference.annotation: The primary text the entry directly narrates — Petrarch's imaginary dialogue with Augustine that dramatizes, without resolving, the tension between classical learning and Christian piety central to the entry's account of his modernity.

reference.author: Cicero, Marcus Tullius
reference.title: Letters to Atticus (Epistulae ad Atticum)
reference.annotation: The lost-and-recovered primary document at the heart of the entry's central episode — Petrarch's 1345 discovery of these letters revealed a politically anxious, human Cicero and crystallized his sense of historical distance.

reference.author: Vickers, Nancy J.
reference.title: Diana Described: Scattered Woman and Scattered Rhyme
reference.annotation: The source of the entry's feminist critique of the Petrarchan love convention — Vickers's argument that the beloved is rendered as scattered, depersonalized parts underpins the contested-inheritance reading of Laura as 'a list of beautiful parts.'

commerce.title: Petrarch: A Critical Guide to the Complete Works
commerce.author: Victoria Kirkham and Armando Maggi (eds.)
commerce.note: The best accessible scholarly introduction — covers the full range of Petrarch's works with contributions from leading specialists.

commerce.title: The Civilization of the Renaissance in Italy
commerce.author: Jacob Burckhardt
commerce.note: The foundational account — Burckhardt's nineteenth-century synthesis made Petrarch the first modern individual. Read it as intellectual history as well as history.

commerce.title: Selected Letters — Petrarch
commerce.author: Petrarch
commerce.note: The primary source — Petrarch's letters, including those addressed to ancient authors, reveal his intellectual personality and his relationship to the classical past more vividly than any secondary account.

commerce.title: In the Footsteps of the Ancients
commerce.author: Ronald Witt
commerce.note: Essential for understanding how Petrarch's influence spread — Witt traces the development of humanist literary practice from Petrarch through his successors.

commerce.title: Rereading the Renaissance
commerce.author: Carol Quillen
commerce.note: The essential micro-level account of Petrarch's reading practices — for readers who want to understand how he actually engaged with classical texts.

commerce.title: The Crisis of the Early Italian Renaissance
commerce.author: Hans Baron
commerce.note: The essential account of civic humanism — Baron's argument about how Petrarch's literary recovery became a civic program is the most influential twentieth-century thesis about Renaissance culture.

popularCulture.title: The Petrarchan sonnet as literary convention
popularCulture.description: Petrarch's fourteen-line poem became the leading form in European poetry for three hundred years. Spanish, French, English, and Portuguese poets all used it. Shakespeare's Sonnet 130 jokes about a mistress who is nothing like the ideal, and the joke works only because readers knew that ideal. The form is named for Petrarch, though he built it from earlier Italian writers rather than inventing it alone.

popularCulture.title: 'The Dark Ages' as popular historical category
popularCulture.description: Petrarch coined the phrase 'the dark ages' for the centuries after Rome fell, and people still use it. The label is a piece of praise for antiquity dressed up as a description. Those same centuries built Gothic cathedrals and produced Dante. Much of value happened between Rome's fall and Petrarch's own day, so most historians have dropped the phrase even as everyday speech keeps it.

popularCulture.title: Unrequited love as literary genre
popularCulture.description: Petrarch's poems about Laura set a lasting pattern: a man aches for a woman who is lovely, distant, and almost unreal. The shape runs through Shakespeare, Sidney, Spenser, and modern romance. In these poems the woman is barely there. Laura hardly speaks and does little. She mostly mirrors the poet's own longing, a beautiful surface rather than a person with her own inner life.

popularCulture.title: Mont Ventoux and the first modern hike
popularCulture.description: In 1336 Petrarch climbed Mont Ventoux in France and wrote about it. At the top he opened Augustine and felt ashamed for caring more about the view than his soul. People often call this the first account of hiking for pleasure. That claim goes too far. Others before him had walked up mountains and reflected on it. What stands out is his habit of turning every moment into self-examination.

popularCulture.title: The humanist scholar as cultural type
popularCulture.description: Petrarch shaped a familiar figure: the scholar who reads old texts in their first languages, hunts down rare manuscripts, writes long letters to other learned people, and brings ancient wisdom to present problems. University humanities professors still fit the mold. The pattern is more his than anyone's, yet he did not invent it from nothing. Earlier scholars hunted manuscripts and prized ancient learning before him too.

comparativeSummary: Petrarch is usually remembered as the man who saw the past clearly, yet each vantage point here asks whether he discovered something or simply invented it. Historians of the Middle Ages treat his "dark ages" as a polemic that buried real achievements, not a finding. Scholars outside Europe note that dividing time into lost-and-recovered eras is one culture's habit, carried worldwide by power rather than truth. Poets remember a third Petrarch entirely, the maker of a love convention later writers leaned on and mocked. The shared question: when does naming the past become making it up?
